Unit 2: Classification and Diversity of Living Organisms

Learning Competencies

  • State the characteristics of living things
  • Classify living things based on taxonomic principles
  • Argue for or against the importance of classification
  • Describe the system of Linnaean nomenclature
  • Classify common Ethiopian animals and plants based on the taxonomic categories.
  • List the characteristic features of the five kingdoms
  • Appreciate the works of renowned taxonomists in Ethiopia
  • Describe the characteristics of animals.
  • Differentiate between vertebrates and invertebrates.
  • Compare and contrast reproduction in vertebrates.
  • Evaluate the economic importance of animals (insects) in agriculture, food, industry, health, and medicine.
  • Explain animal behavior.
  • Discuss the types and patterns of animal behavior.
  • Discuss homeostasis in animals: thermoregulation, osmoregulation and sugar balance.
  • Appreciate the work of a renowned zoologist in Ethiopia or in your locality.

2.1 Characteristics and Classification of Organisms

2.1.1 Characteristics of Living Things

Living things come in all sorts of shapes and sizes. Biologists study life in many ways, observing wildlife, collecting fossils, or even listening to whales. But what makes something truly "alive"? While it’s easy to tell that a galloping horse is alive and a car isn't, simply moving isn't enough to define life—a car moves, and an airplane flies, but neither is alive. Although we can't capture the essence of life in one sentence, we can identify several key characteristics that all living systems share.

What are the characteristics of living things?

All living things share several fundamental properties:

i. All living things are made up of one or more cells:

Cells are the basic building blocks of life. Organisms made of a single cell, like bacteria, are called unicellular. Those made of many cells, like plants and animals, are called multicellular.

Example 1: Unicellular Organism

Amoebas are tiny, single-celled organisms found in water. They perform all life functions within that one cell, like moving, eating, and reproducing.

Example 2: Multicellular Organism

Humans are multicellular organisms made up of trillions of cells, which are organized into tissues, organs, and organ systems, all working together.

ii. All living things require energy:

Every organism needs energy for its metabolic activities. For example, the energy that powers your muscles comes from the food you eat. Some organisms, like plants, produce their own food using sunlight through photosynthesis; these are known as producers or autotrophs. Other organisms, called consumers or heterotrophs, cannot make their own food and must consume other organisms.

Example 1: Autotroph

A sunflower plant uses sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to produce its own sugars (food) through photosynthesis.

Example 2: Heterotroph

A lion hunts and eats other animals, like zebras, to obtain the energy it needs to survive.

iii. All living organisms respond to stimuli:

Organisms can detect and react to changes (stimuli) in their internal or external environment and make appropriate responses.

Example 1: External Stimulus

When you touch a hot stove, your hand quickly pulls away. The heat is the stimulus, and pulling your hand away is the response.

Example 2: Internal Stimulus

When your body temperature rises, you sweat to cool down. The rising temperature is an internal stimulus.

iv. All living things can grow:

Growth is a permanent increase in size and mass, resulting from an increase in cell number, cell size, or both. Even bacteria grow in size. Multicellular organisms become more complex and change shape as their cell numbers increase.

Example 1: Unicellular Growth

A single bacterium grows in size and then divides into two new bacteria, increasing its mass and cell count.

Example 2: Multicellular Growth

A human baby grows into an adult by increasing the number and size of its cells, leading to a significant increase in height and weight.

v. All living things can reproduce:

Reproduction is the process by which organisms create more of their own kind. Single-celled organisms might simply divide into two. Multicellular plants and animals can reproduce sexually or asexually.

Example 1: Asexual Reproduction

A starfish can regenerate a whole new body from a single arm, a form of asexual reproduction.

Example 2: Sexual Reproduction

Birds lay eggs that hatch into chicks, which grow into adult birds capable of reproducing themselves, involving the combination of genetic material from two parents.

vi. All living things can excrete:

Excretion is the removal of metabolic waste products generated by chemical reactions (metabolism) within cells. For example, respiration produces carbon dioxide, which living organisms expel from their bodies in various ways.

Example 1: Animal Excretion

Humans exhale carbon dioxide, a waste product of cellular respiration, and excrete urine to remove other metabolic wastes.

Example 2: Plant Excretion

Plants release oxygen as a byproduct of photosynthesis, and some store waste products in their leaves which are shed.

vii. All living things display ordered complexity:

Living things are both complex and highly organized. Biological systems are organized in hierarchical levels, starting from atoms and molecules, and increasing in complexity. For instance, your body has many different types of cells, each containing intricate molecular structures. While many non-living things can be complex, they don't show the same level of ordered complexity.

Example 1: Cellular Organization

A single human cell contains numerous organelles like mitochondria, the nucleus, and endoplasmic reticulum, all arranged in a specific, highly ordered manner to perform specialized functions.

Example 2: Ecosystem Complexity

A forest ecosystem exhibits ordered complexity with various organisms (plants, animals, fungi, bacteria) interacting in a structured food web, and with distinct physical layers (canopy, understory, forest floor).

viii. Most living things maintain homeostasis:

Most organisms maintain stable internal conditions that differ from their external environment. Homeostasis is the regulation of an organism's internal environment to maintain stability. For example, your body temperature stays constant even when outside temperatures change.

Example 1: Body Temperature Regulation

When you exercise, your body sweats to release heat and maintain a core temperature of around 37°C, preventing overheating.

Example 2: Blood Sugar Regulation

After eating, your body releases insulin to bring elevated blood sugar levels back down to a healthy range, maintaining glucose homeostasis.

ix. All living things possess adaptations that evolve over time:

Organisms interact with each other and their environment in ways that affect their survival. This leads to the evolution of adaptations, which are features that help organisms thrive in their specific environments.

Example 1: Camouflage

A chameleon changes its skin color to blend in with its surroundings, an adaptation that helps it hide from predators and ambush prey.

Example 2: Bird Beaks

Finches on the Galápagos Islands have evolved different beak shapes, each adapted to efficiently crack a specific type of seed found in their habitat.

2.1.2 Taxonomy of Living Things

2.1.2.1 Principles of Classification

Why do biologists classify living things?

Organizing items makes them easier to find and understand. For biologists, a key tool for organizing and understanding living organisms is classification. This is the process of grouping things based on their similarities. The science of naming, identifying, and classifying organisms is known as taxonomy. Scientists who study taxonomy are called taxonomists.

Biologists classify organisms into different categories primarily by assessing their apparent similarities and differences. This includes examining their external and internal structures, as well as their habitat. Taxonomists also analyze the genetic makeup of organisms to uncover their evolutionary relationships. The assumption is that greater physical similarity often indicates a closer biological relationship. They aim to identify and classify organisms using various features, such as morphological (form and structure), physiological (functions), molecular (DNA/RNA), behavioral, and ecological characteristics.

2.1.2.2 Taxonomic Hierarchies in Biological Classification

Thousands of years ago, the Greek philosopher Aristotle (384-322 BC) developed some of the earliest widely accepted biological classification systems. He categorized plants into trees, shrubs, and herbs based on simple physical characteristics. He also divided animals into two groups: those with red blood and those without. While this system was useful for a time, Aristotle sometimes grouped organisms with very little in common, like birds, bats, and flying insects, simply because they could all fly.

Later, in the 1700s, Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus (1707-1778), often called the father of taxonomy, revolutionized classification. He introduced a taxonomic hierarchy and proposed an orderly system for classifying organisms.

A taxonomic hierarchy is a system for arranging various organisms into successive levels of biological classification, either in decreasing or increasing order. In Linnaeus's system, all organisms are placed in a ranked hierarchy. The highest rank is Kingdom, followed by Phylum (or division for plants), Class, Order, Family, Genus, and Species. Each rank within this hierarchy is called a taxon (plural: taxa).

Linnaeus initially developed a two-kingdom system, classifying all living organisms under Kingdom Plantae (plants) and Kingdom Animalia (animals). However, this system didn't differentiate between eukaryotes and prokaryotes, unicellular and multicellular organisms, or photosynthetic organisms (like green algae) and non-photosynthetic ones (like fungi).

At the broadest level, biologists now divide all life into three domains: Bacteria, Archaea, and Eukarya. The domain is a rank above the kingdom.

Every organism on Earth belongs to one of these three domains. The first two domains, Bacteria and Archaea, include two distinct groups of organisms with prokaryotic cells, which are relatively small, simple cells lacking a nucleus or other membrane-bound compartments. Archaea are prokaryotic, lacking a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles, and many thrive in extreme environments. Bacteria are also prokaryotic, lacking a nucleus, and are generally small organisms.

Major differences between bacteria and archaea include the chemical composition of their cell walls and cell membranes. Bacteria use chlorophyll for photosynthesis, while archaea do not. Archaea can generate methane, and bacteria are sensitive to some antibiotics. Eukaryotes, on the other hand, have relatively large and complex cells that contain a nucleus and other membrane-enclosed compartments. They are grouped into the domain Eukarya, which includes diverse groups like protists, fungi, plants, and animals, with forms ranging from unicellular to colonial and multicellular organisms.

Archaea Bacteria Eukarya
Cell Structure Cells with no membrane-bound organelles. Cells with no nucleus; no membrane-bound organelles. Cells with a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.
DNA Circular 'chromosome'. Circular 'chromosome'. DNA in the nucleus arranged as linear chromosomes with histone proteins.
Ribosomes Similar to bacteria. Similar to archaea. Different from both bacteria and archaea.
Cell Wall Does not contain peptidoglycans. Contains peptidoglycans. Varied (e.g., cellulose in plants, chitin in fungi).
Cell Division By binary fission. By binary fission. By mitosis.
Organism Type Unicellular/colonial. Unicellular/colonial. Unicellular, colonial, and multicellular.
Reproduction Asexual. Asexual. Many different ways (asexually and sexually).
Taxonomic Hierarchy: Humans (Homo sapiens)
Rank Description/Characteristics
Species: Homo sapiens Bi-pedal and higher intelligence
Genus: Homo Hominids with large brain & upright posture
Family: Hominidae Primates with relatively flat faces & binocular vision
Order: Primata Mammals with forward-looking eyes & grasping fingers
Class: Mammalia Body covered with hair/fur. Mammary glands present
Phylum: Chordata Animals with a notochord or vertebral column (backbone)
Kingdom: Animalia

2.1.3 Relevance of Classification

What are the relevancies of biological classification?

Biological classification has many important uses. It helps us identify harmful and beneficial organisms, saves time and energy when studying organisms, and allows us to understand the relationships between different life forms. Classification provides biologists with a framework for studying the connections between organisms. For instance, this framework helps us explore the relationship between birds and dinosaurs. Biologists have found that some dinosaur bones have large internal spaces, similar to bird bones. This similarity suggests that dinosaurs may be more closely related to birds than to other reptiles.

Taxonomy is also valuable for scientists in fields like agriculture, forestry, and medicine, and it can even boost the economy. For example, if a taxonomist knows that a certain tree species contains chemicals useful as disinfectants (like Shiferaw or Moringa stenopetala), they can then look for related plant species that might contain the same useful substances, potentially providing multiple sources for those chemicals.

Did you know Carl Linnaeus?

Carolus Linnaeus (1707–1778) was a Swedish naturalist who introduced the hierarchical system of classification. He is often called the "father of taxonomy" for his work in classifying plants and animals. His major contribution was the development of binomial nomenclature, a system for giving organisms two-part scientific names.

Key Terms

  • Identification: The process of recognizing organisms using their unique features.
  • Nomenclature: The part of taxonomy that specifically deals with naming organisms.
  • Taxonomy: The scientific field dedicated to naming and classifying species.

2.1.4 Linnaean System of Nomenclature

How did Carl Linnaeus classify living organisms?

Carl Linnaeus, a Swedish naturalist, initially studied medicine but developed a deep interest in plants. He traveled extensively in Scandinavia, England, and Eastern Europe, discovering and naming many new plant species. In 1735, he published his influential work, Systema Naturae, where he accurately described and classified 7,700 plant species. He further organized species into genera, genera into classes, and classes into orders. He also classified over 4,000 animals, although his animal classification into mammals, birds, insects, and worms was less refined.

Linnaeus refined and popularized the binomial system of naming organisms. In this system, the first name represents the genus, and the second name is the specific epithet. This system remains the official starting point for naming or revising the names of organisms worldwide.

Binomial nomenclature

Species must have names that are recognized globally to avoid confusion. For example, maize is known by various local names like Bekolo in Amharic, Boqqollo (Badallaa) in Afan Oromo, Baddela in Sidaamu Afoo, and "corn" in American English. Such diverse names can lead to confusion. However, using the botanical name, Zea mays, prevents any misunderstanding. Given the cultural and linguistic diversity in countries like Ethiopia, with over 80 ethnolinguistic groups, it would be extremely difficult to know all the local names for a species. Similarly, Africa alone has thousands of languages. Therefore, the Latin form of scientific names allows them to be used universally, overcoming language barriers. These scientific names also help differentiate between species that might look similar in size, shape, behavior, or color, thereby avoiding confusion.

The binomial system of naming species, introduced by Linnaeus, is an internationally agreed-upon system where an organism's scientific name consists of two parts: the genus and the species.

The term "binomial" means 'two names'. The first name identifies the genus (plural: genera), which is a group of similar species. The second name, the specific epithet, often describes a characteristic of the organism. For example, Enset ventricosum (false banana) and Musa acuminata (banana) both belong to the same family (Musaceae).

When writing a scientific name, the first letter of the genus name is capitalized, while the specific epithet always starts with a small letter. For instance, the scientific name for humans is Homo sapiens. Scientific names are always underlined when handwritten or italicized when printed.

Example 1: Dog Scientific Name

The scientific name for the domestic dog is Canis familiaris. "Canis" is the genus, and "familiaris" is the specific epithet. This two-part name ensures clarity and universal recognition.

2.1.5 Common Ethiopian Animals and Plants

Ethiopia boasts high biological diversity (biodiversity) due to its unique geographical location, varied topography, and diverse climate. It's a hotspot for wild plant and animal species, many of which are endemic (found nowhere else). Ethiopia is also a primary center of diversity for several field crops, including noug (Guizotia abyssinica), tef (Eragrostis tef), and Ethiopian mustard (Brassica carinata). Additionally, crops like barley, sorghum, durum wheat, finger millet, faba bean, chickpea, lentil, and cowpea show wide diversity in Ethiopia.

Dichotomous keys

How do biologists use dichotomous keys?

Dichotomous keys are tools used to identify unfamiliar organisms, simplifying the identification process. Each key consists of pairs of contrasting features (dichotomous means two branches), starting with general characteristics and progressing to more specific ones. By following the key and making appropriate choices, you can correctly identify an organism.

For example, imagine a dichotomous key for vertebrates and invertebrates. Item 1 might ask if the animal is poikilothermic (cold-blooded) or homoiothermic (warm-blooded). If it’s cold-blooded, you move to item 2; if it’s warm-blooded, you move to item 4. This technique can be applied to classify an organism down to its class, genus, or species. Sometimes, the most important features might not be easy to observe, requiring the use of less fundamental characteristics.

2.1.6 The Five-Kingdom System of Classification

Why did we come up with the five kingdoms?

Classifying organisms simply as plants or animals was easy to understand, but many organisms didn't fit neatly into either category. The two-kingdom classification, though used for a long time, proved inadequate.

In 1969, Whittaker proposed a five-kingdom classification to address the shortcomings of the two-kingdom system. His main criteria for classification included cell structure, body organization, mode of nutrition, reproduction, and phylogenetic relationships. Whittaker's five-kingdom scheme includes Animals, Plants, Fungi, Monera, and Protists. However, it's still challenging to fit all organisms into this scheme. For instance, many protists with chlorophyll (protophyta) closely resemble some algae, but algae are classified within the plant kingdom.

Viruses are not included in any kingdom because they are generally not considered living organisms. They lack key characteristics of life; they don't grow, develop, or carry out respiration. Viruses are particles composed of nucleic acids (DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat. They are much smaller than bacteria. All viruses replicate, but they require a living host cell to do so. The cell in which a virus replicates is called the host cell.

2.1.6.1 Kingdom Monera

What are Monera?

The Kingdom Monera includes eubacteria (true bacteria) and archaebacteria (archaea). Eubacteria have strong cell walls and come in various shapes and forms, such as spherical (cocci), rod-shaped (bacilli), and spiral (spirilla).

Some eubacteria are heterotrophs, meaning they consume other organisms for food. Others are autotrophs, capable of making their own food. Among autotrophic bacteria, some perform photosynthesis like plants, while others perform chemosynthesis, deriving energy from chemical reactions. Eubacteria live in most habitats, excluding the most extreme ones. While some eubacteria cause diseases like strep throat and pneumonia, most are harmless and helpful.

Archaebacteria (Archaea) have cell walls that differ significantly from those of bacteria. Like bacteria, archaebacteria make their own food through chemosynthesis and photosynthesis. They typically thrive in extreme environments, such as swamps, deep-ocean hot-water vents, and evaporating seawater ponds, often in oxygen-free conditions.

Shape Description Example
Spherical Rounded or oval-shaped cells. Cocci (e.g., Staphylococcus aureus)
Rod-shaped Elongated, cylindrical cells. Bacilli (e.g., Escherichia coli)
Spiral Helical or curved cells. Spirillum (e.g., Helicobacter pylori)

Important characteristics of monera:

  • They are simple prokaryotic unicellular organisms.
  • They lack a well-defined nucleus or any membrane-bound organelles.
  • Most have a rigid cell wall.
  • They have various modes of nutrition, including autotrophs or heterotrophs.
  • They are known as decomposers and mineralizers in the biosphere, playing crucial roles in nutrient cycling.

Example 1: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria

Bacteria like Rhizobium, found in the root nodules of legumes, convert atmospheric nitrogen into a usable form for plants. This is a crucial role as nitrogen is an essential nutrient for plant growth.

Example 2: Cyanobacteria

Also known as blue-green algae, cyanobacteria are photosynthetic autotrophs that produce oxygen and form the base of many aquatic food chains. They can be found in freshwater, marine, and even terrestrial environments.

2.1.6.2 Kingdom Protista

What are protists?

There is no single "typical" protist. The Kingdom Protista contains the most diverse organisms among all kingdoms. They can be single-celled (unicellular) or many-celled (multicellular). Some are microscopic, while others are very large. Some make their own food, others do not. The one common feature among all protists is that they are all eukaryotes, meaning most of their metabolic processes occur within membrane-bound organelles.

Beyond this shared eukaryotic nature, protists are quite varied. Some, called protozoans, are animal-like but consist of only one cell. Others, known as algae, are plant-like but lack roots, stems, or leaves. Algae are photosynthetic and autotrophic, and unicellular algae form the base of aquatic food chains while producing a significant amount of Earth's oxygen. Still other protists resemble fungi but do not have the same type of cell walls as fungi.

Example 1: Paramecium (Protozoan)

A Paramecium is a single-celled protozoan that moves using tiny hair-like structures called cilia. It lives in freshwater and feeds on bacteria and other small organisms.

Example 2: Euglena (Alga)

A Euglena is a unicellular alga that can perform photosynthesis like a plant, but can also consume food like an animal. It moves using a whip-like tail called a flagellum.

Key Terms

  • Protozoans: Animal-like protists, typically unicellular and heterotrophic.
  • Algae: Plant-like protists that are photosynthetic and can be unicellular or multicellular.

2.1.6.3 Kingdom Fungi

What are fungi?

Fungi are eukaryotic organisms that include microorganisms like yeasts and molds, as well as macroscopic organisms like mushrooms. While most yeasts are unicellular, most fungi are filamentous multicellular organisms.

Their bodies are typically composed of long, slender, thread-like structures called hyphae, which are crucial for how fungi obtain food.

Fungi have a cell wall made of chitin and polysaccharides. Like animals, fungi are heterotrophic in nutrition, meaning they do not produce their own food. However, unlike animals, fungi do not ingest (eat) their food. Instead, a fungus absorbs nutrients from its environment after secreting powerful enzymes that break down complex organic molecules into smaller, absorbable compounds. These enzymes allow fungi to digest a wide range of sources, whether living or dead.

Most fungi are saprophytes, meaning they absorb soluble organic matter from dead substrates and are thus important decomposers. Decomposer fungi break down and absorb nutrients from non-living organic materials like fallen logs, animal carcasses, and waste products from living organisms, playing a vital role in nutrient recycling.

Fungi can also exist in different symbiotic relationships:

  • Parasites: Some fungi are parasitic, absorbing nutrients from the cells of living hosts. Pathogenic parasitic fungi can cause diseases in humans (e.g., Candida albicans causes thrush, and Tinea pedis causes athlete's foot), animals, and plants.
  • Mutualists: Mutualistic fungi also absorb nutrients from a host but provide benefits in return. For example, some fungi living inside termites help break down wood, making food available for the termites. Fungi can also form symbiotic associations with algae as lichens and with plant roots as mycorrhiza.

Fungi can reproduce both asexually (through fragmentation, fission, budding, or spore formation) and sexually, with spores produced in distinct structures called fruiting bodies.

Yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) is a very important fungus used in baking to make injera rise and in brewing to produce alcohol (like Tej, Tella, and Beer).

Example 1: Mushroom (Basidiomycota)

Mushrooms are the fruiting bodies of many types of fungi. They play a crucial role as decomposers in forests, breaking down dead wood and leaf litter, returning nutrients to the soil.

Example 2: Penicillium (Ascomycota)

Penicillium is a mold that is famous for producing the antibiotic penicillin, which has revolutionized medicine. Some species are also used in cheese production.

Key Terms

  • Mutualism: A symbiotic relationship where both species benefit.
  • Parasitism: A symbiotic relationship where one organism benefits at the expense of another.
  • Symbiosis: A permanent, close association between two or more organisms of different species.
  • Hyphae: Long, slender, thread-like structures that make up the body of most fungi.

2.1.6.4 Kingdom Plantae

What are plants?

Kingdom Plantae includes all eukaryotic, multicellular, and autotrophic organisms. Plants produce their own food through photosynthesis, using chloroplasts and chlorophyll pigment. This process also releases oxygen into the atmosphere.

Key characteristics of plants include:

  • Some are parasitic.
  • Nearly all live on land.
  • Sexual reproduction predominates.
  • They are stationary and do not move from place to place.
  • Their cells contain a rigid cell wall made up of cellulose.
  • They reproduce asexually by vegetative propagation or sexually.

There are over 250,000 species of plants, including flowering plants, mosses, ferns, and coniferous plants.

Group Characteristics Examples
Bryophyta Non-vascular, small, live in moist environments, reproduce via spores. Mosses, Liverworts, Hornworts
Pteridophyta Vascular tissue, reproduce via spores, have true roots, stems, and leaves. Ferns
Gymnospermae Vascular tissue, "naked" seeds (not enclosed in an ovary), produce cones. Conifers (pines, spruces, firs), Cycads
Angiospermae Vascular tissue, seeds enclosed within an ovary (fruit), produce flowers, most diverse group. Flowering plants (roses, oak trees, grasses)

Example 1: Oak Tree (Angiosperm)

An oak tree is a flowering plant (angiosperm) that produces acorns, which are fruits containing its seeds. It is multicellular and autotrophic, performing photosynthesis to create its own food.

Example 2: Sphagnum Moss (Bryophyte)

Sphagnum moss is a non-vascular plant that lacks true roots and stems. It grows in damp, boggy areas and plays an important ecological role in peat formation.

2.1.6.5 Kingdom Animalia

What is an Animal?

Kingdom Animalia includes all multicellular, heterotrophic, and eukaryotic organisms. Defining an animal isn't always straightforward because there are exceptions to nearly every criterion used to distinguish them from other life forms. However, a combination of characteristics generally defines animals.

Animals differ from both plants and fungi in their mode of nutrition. Unlike plants, animals are not photosynthetic. Animals consume food obtained from other organisms (they are heterotrophs). But unlike fungi, most animals do not feed by absorption; instead, animals ingest their food and then use enzymes to digest it internally.

In contrast to plants and fungi, animals lack structural support from cell walls. Instead, animal cells are held together by structural proteins, with collagen being the most abundant and unique to animals.

Many animals possess two specialized cell types not found in other multicellular organisms: muscle cells and nerve cells. In most animals, these cells are organized into muscle tissue and nervous tissue, respectively. They are responsible for body movement and conducting nerve impulses. This ability to move and transmit nerve impulses is fundamental to many adaptations that distinguish animals from plants and fungi.

2.2 Characteristics of Animals

Animals are an incredibly diverse group of organisms, ranging from microscopic creatures to colossal whales. Despite their vast differences, they share several fundamental characteristics that set them apart from other life forms like plants and fungi.

Key Characteristics of Animals

  • Eukaryotic: Animal cells have a true nucleus and other membrane-bound organelles.
  • Multicellular: Animals are made up of many cells that work together, forming tissues, organs, and organ systems.
  • Heterotrophic: Animals cannot produce their own food; they obtain nutrients by consuming other organisms.
  • Sensitive to Stimuli: Animals can detect and respond to changes in their environment.
  • Reproduction: Most animals reproduce sexually, but some can reproduce asexually.
  • Motility: Most animals are capable of movement at some stage of their life cycle.
  • Respiration: Animals perform cellular respiration to release energy from food.
  • Excretion: Animals remove waste products from their bodies.
  • Growth: Animals grow and develop over time.
  • Protection: Animals have various mechanisms to protect themselves from predators and environmental threats.
  • Body Symmetry: Animals exhibit different types of body symmetry, such as radial, bilateral, or asymmetry.

While these are common traits, animals also have distinguishing characteristics that allow for their classification into different groups.

Animals are highly diverse and are generally classified into two main groups based on the presence or absence of a backbone:

i. Vertebrates and Invertebrates

Animals are broadly categorized into two major groups based on the presence or absence of a backbone (vertebral column):

  • Vertebrates: These animals possess a backbone. This group includes fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals.
  • Invertebrates: These animals do not have a backbone. This diverse group includes sponges, jellyfish, worms, insects, snails, and starfish.
Vertebrates (With Backbone) Invertebrates (Without Backbone)
Mammals: Have fur/hair, mammary glands, warm-blooded, live birth. Porifera (Sponges): Simple, sessile, filter feeders, no true tissues.
Fish: Live underwater, gills for breathing, scales, cold-blooded, lay eggs. Platyhelminthes (Flatworms): Flat bodies, bilateral symmetry, no body cavity.
Birds: Feathers, wings, toothless beaks, warm-blooded, lay eggs. Annelida (Segmented Worms): Segmented bodies, bilateral symmetry, bristles.
Reptiles: Scales or shells, cold-blooded, lay eggs, cannot chew. Echinodermata (Starfish, Sea Urchins): Spiny skin, radial symmetry, marine.
Amphibians: Thin/humid skin, transformation (gills to lungs), cold-blooded, lay eggs, live on land and water. Cnidaria (Jellyfish, Corals): Stinging cells, radial symmetry, aquatic.
Mollusca (Snails, Clams, Octopuses): Soft bodies, often with a shell, muscular foot.
Arthropoda (Insects, Spiders, Crustaceans): Exoskeleton, jointed legs, segmented body.

Example 1: Jellyfish (Invertebrate - Cnidaria)

A jellyfish is an invertebrate with a soft, bell-shaped body and stinging tentacles. It lives in water and lacks a backbone, relying on its watery environment for support.

Example 2: Eagle (Vertebrate - Birds)

An eagle is a vertebrate, possessing a strong backbone and an internal skeleton. It has feathers for flight, lays eggs, and is warm-blooded, showcasing characteristics typical of birds.

ii. Poikilothermic and Homeothermic Animals

Vertebrates can be further classified based on how they regulate their body temperature:

  • Homeothermic (Warm-blooded) Animals: These animals maintain a constant internal body temperature regardless of the external environment. This group includes mammals and birds.
  • Poikilothermic (Cold-blooded) Animals: These animals have a body temperature that fluctuates with the environmental temperature. This group includes fish, reptiles, and amphibians.

Understanding Body Temperature Regulation

Example 1: A Bird's Constant Temperature

A robin maintains a stable body temperature of around 40-42°C, whether it's a chilly morning or a warm afternoon. It uses internal physiological processes, like shivering or panting, to regulate its temperature. This makes the robin a homeothermic animal.

Example 2: A Lizard's Changing Temperature

A lizard basking on a rock in the sun will have a higher body temperature than when it hides in the shade on a cooler day. Its body temperature largely depends on the external environment. This makes the lizard a poikilothermic animal.

2.2.1 Reproduction in Animals

Reproduction is a fundamental process that ensures the continuity of animal species. It is how living organisms create new individuals. Animals exhibit two main types of reproduction: asexual and sexual.

i. Asexual Reproduction in Animals

Asexual reproduction involves a single individual producing offspring that are genetically identical to itself. This type of reproduction does not require the fusion of gametes (sperm and egg) from two parents. It is more common among invertebrates than vertebrates.

Common Forms of Asexual Reproduction:

  • Budding: A new organism develops from an outgrowth or bud due to cell division at one particular site. For example, hydra can reproduce by budding.
  • Fragmentation: An organism breaks into two or more fragments, and each fragment develops into a new individual. For example, some species of starfish can regenerate from a single arm.
  • Parthenogenesis: An unfertilized egg develops into a new individual. This occurs in some insects (like honeybees) and even a few vertebrates.

ii. Sexual Reproduction in Animals

Sexual reproduction involves two individual parents (male and female) and requires the fusion of gametes to produce offspring. The offspring inherit genetic material from both parents, resulting in genetic diversity. The parents are diploid organisms (2n chromosomes), and their gametes are haploid (n chromosomes).

Males produce sperm (haploid cells) in the testes, which are stored in the epididymis until ejaculation. Females produce an ovum or egg (haploid cell) that matures in the ovary. The fusion of sperm and egg during fertilization creates a diploid cell called a zygote (2n), which then develops into an embryo and eventually an adult organism.

Types of Fertilization:

  • External Fertilization: Eggs and sperm are released into the external environment (usually water) where fertilization occurs. This is common in aquatic animals like frogs and many fish.
  • Internal Fertilization: Sperm are deposited inside the female's reproductive tract, and fertilization occurs within the female's body. This is common in terrestrial animals, including insects, reptiles, birds, and mammals, as it protects the gametes from dehydration.

iii. Embryonic Development

After fertilization, the zygote undergoes a series of developmental stages:

  • Cleavage: The zygote undergoes rapid mitotic cell divisions, increasing the number of cells without significantly increasing the overall size of the embryo. This typically results in an eight-celled structure.
  • Blastulation: The cells continue to divide and rearrange to form a hollow ball of cells called a blastula.
  • Gastrulation: The blastula undergoes further cell division and rearrangement, forming a multilayered structure called a gastrula. This process establishes the primary germ layers: the ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm.
  • Organogenesis: The germ layers differentiate and develop into different tissue types, organs, and organ systems, eventually forming a complete adult organism.

iv. Reproduction in Insects (Complete and Incomplete Metamorphosis)

Insects, the most diverse group of animals, are arthropods with segmented bodies, jointed legs, and an external skeleton (exoskeleton). They undergo sexual reproduction and have distinct life cycles involving metamorphosis.

Metamorphosis:

This refers to the series of major changes in body structure that insects undergo during development. There are two types:

  • Complete Metamorphosis: Involves four distinct stages: egg, larva, pupa, and adult. Each stage looks very different from the others.
  • Incomplete Metamorphosis: Involves three stages: egg, nymph, and adult. The nymph resembles a smaller version of the adult, gradually growing and molting several times.

v. Reproduction in Frogs

Frogs are semi-aquatic amphibians that undergo sexual reproduction with external fertilization. Unlike birds, they do not produce amniotic eggs. Instead, their eggs are typically covered in a jelly-like substance and must be laid in water to prevent drying out.

After fertilization, frogs undergo metamorphosis. The fertilized eggs develop into a larval stage called a tadpole, which is very different from the adult frog. Tadpoles are aquatic, typically have gills for breathing, and a tail for swimming. They gradually transform into adult frogs, losing their gills and tail, and developing lungs and legs.

A tadpole swims using its tail and breathes with gills. Over several weeks, its tail shortens, legs develop, and lungs replace gills, allowing it to transition from an aquatic tadpole to a terrestrial adult frog.

vi. Reproduction in Crocodiles

Crocodiles are large semi-aquatic reptiles that reproduce sexually with internal fertilization. Unlike frogs, crocodiles lay hard, leathery eggs, providing better protection for the developing young. They bury their eggs in sand or mound vegetation.

The number of eggs laid varies by species, typically ranging from 10 to 100. Crocodiles exhibit some parental care by guarding their nests, which is an advancement over frogs that typically abandon their eggs after laying.

Crocodile Reproduction and Parental Care

Example 1: Nest Building and Protection

A female crocodile digs a nest near water, lays her eggs, and then covers them with vegetation or sand. She remains near the nest, often fiercely guarding it from predators until the eggs hatch.

Example 2: Leathery Eggs

Imagine the tough, resilient shell of a crocodile egg. This leathery texture is a key adaptation for terrestrial egg-laying, providing superior protection against physical damage and dehydration compared to the jelly-like eggs of amphibians.

vii. Reproduction in Birds

Birds reproduce sexually with internal fertilization and lay hard-shelled eggs. Most bird species are monogamous, meaning a single male and female mate for an entire breeding season, while others are polygamous, having multiple partners.

Reproduction begins with the ovum (egg) being fertilized by sperm within the female's oviduct. The fertilized egg then travels down the oviduct, where layers of albumen (egg white) and shell membranes are added, followed by the hard shell. Birds then lay their eggs in nests, which they incubate using their body heat. The incubation period allows the embryo to develop fully.

Hatching involves the chick using a temporary "egg tooth" on its beak to break through the shell. Parental care in birds is varied, ranging from species where chicks are self-sufficient soon after hatching to those where parents provide extended care, including feeding and protection in nests.

Parts of the Oviduct Nature and Functions
Infundibulum A funnel-shaped upper portion of the oviduct. Its purpose is to search out and engulf the yolk, causing it to enter the oviduct.
Magnum The longest part of the oviduct. Secretion of albumen: nearly all the egg white is deposited in the magnum.
Isthmus The relatively short portion of the oviduct. Formation of shell membrane—inner and outer shell membranes. The glands of the isthmus produce sulfur-containing amino acids important for shell membrane formation.
Uterus Developing an egg takes a longer period of time. Formation of eggshell—shell is formed over shell membranes.
Vagina The final section of the oviduct. During oviposition, relaxation of muscles allows the egg to leave the uterus and be laid through the cloaca.

viii. Reproduction in Rats

Rats, as mammals, reproduce sexually with internal fertilization, and their embryos develop internally within the mother during a gestation period (pregnancy). The gestation period varies by species but typically ranges from 22 to 49 days.

After fertilization, the zygote undergoes cleavage, forming a blastocyst (a hollow ball of cells). The blastocyst implants in the uterine horns and differentiates into embryonic and extra-embryonic tissues. The embryo is nourished by the mother through a complex system of blood vessels, including the umbilical cord and placenta, which transport oxygen and nutrients and remove waste. The amniotic sac protects the embryo during development.

Rats typically give birth to a litter of 7 to 12 hairless, deaf offspring with sealed eyelids. Parental care in rats involves building nests and nursing the young (pups or kittens) until they are weaned, usually around 45 days. Female rats often care for pups regardless of their biological mother, and male rats do not typically participate in parental care.

2.2.2 The Economic Importance of Animals (Insects)

Insects, despite often being seen as pests, have significant economic importance, both beneficial and harmful, in agriculture, food production, industry, health, and medicine.

i. Beneficial Aspects of Insects

a. Agriculture:

  • Pollinators: Flower-visiting insects (like bees, butterflies, and hoverflies) are crucial for the reproduction of most flowering plants, including many cultivated crops. They transfer male gametes (pollen) to female gametes, leading to seed and fruit production. An estimated 35% of global crop production relies on insect pollination, which has immense economic value.
  • Pest Regulation (Biological Control): Many insects are natural predators or parasitoids of other insects, especially agricultural pests. This "natural biological control" helps limit pest populations without relying on chemical pesticides.

b. Food:

  • Many insect species are consumed as food by people in various cultures, especially in Asia and Central America. Insects like crickets, grasshoppers, beetle larvae, moth larvae, and termites are rich sources of protein and are considered a sustainable food source.
  • Insect farming is a promising way to address global food security for a growing population due to insects' quick reproduction, high growth rates, and nutritional value.

c. Industry:

  • Honey and Bee Wax: Honeybees produce honey, a valuable food product, and beeswax, used in candles, cosmetics, and other industries.
  • Silk Production: Silkworms (e.g., Bombyx mori) produce silk fibers, which are woven into luxurious textiles.
  • Shellac Production: Lac insects (e.g., Laccifer lacca) secrete shellac, a resin used in dyes, inks, polishes, and sealing waxes.
  • Cochineal Production: Cochineal pigment, extracted from scale insects (e.g., Dactylopius coccus), is used as a natural dye in foods, beverages, cosmetics (lipsticks), and art.
  • Tannic Acid Production: Tiny wasps in the family Cynipidae induce gall tissues on trees that contain tannic acid, a chemical compound used in dyeing leather and manufacturing inks.

Industrial Products from Insects

Example 1: Natural Silk Fabric

The delicate and luxurious silk fabric used for high-end clothing is produced by silkworms. These larvae spin cocoons made of silk threads, which are then harvested and processed.

Example 2: Red Food Coloring

The vibrant red color often seen in certain yogurts, candies, and fruit drinks can sometimes come from cochineal, a natural dye derived from the cochineal scale insect.

d. Health and Medicine:

  • Some insects and their products have medicinal value. Honey is used to treat burns, wounds, and as an immune system booster.
  • Bee and ant venom are used in some treatments for joint pain.
  • Blister beetles secrete cantharidin, a protein blocker effective against some viral infections and potentially useful in cancer treatment.
  • Ground grasshoppers are used in some African cultures as poultices for pain relief, especially migraines.

ii. Harmful Aspects of Insects

While many insects are beneficial, others can be harmful to humans, animals, and crops.

a. Agricultural Pests:

  • Insects like locusts, caterpillars, bugs, hoppers, and aphids feed on various parts of green plants and stored grains, causing significant crop damage and reducing agricultural production.
  • Desert locusts, in particular, can form massive swarms that devour entire fields, leading to food shortages.
  • Pests of stored cereal grains include the Rice Weevil, Lesser Grain Borer, Rust Red Flour Beetle, Sawtooth Grain Beetle, and Flat Grain Beetle.

b. Disease Vectors:

  • Several insects act as vectors, transmitting diseases or pathogens from one organism to another.
  • Anopheles mosquitoes transmit malarial parasites (Plasmodium).
  • Culex mosquitoes spread filarial worms, causing diseases like filariasis.
  • The tsetse fly (Trypanosoma gambiense) spreads African sleeping sickness.
  • Houseflies (Musca domestica) can spread food and water-borne diseases to human populations.


2.2.3 Animal Behavior

Animal behavior encompasses all the ways animals interact with other organisms and their physical environment. This includes movements, social interactions, learning, and responses to various stimuli, all crucial for survival and reproduction.

i. Types of Animal Behavior

Animal behavior can be broadly categorized into two main types: innate (inherent) and learned (acquired) behavior.

a. Innate or Inherent Behavior

Innate behavior is inborn, genetically determined, and independent of experience. It is specific to a species and often occurs instinctively.

Types of Innate Behavior:

  • Instinctive Behaviors: Complex, unlearned behaviors that are genetically programmed.
    • Example 1: Spiders inherently know how to spin intricate webs without being taught.
    • Example 2: Birds have an innate ability to build nests characteristic of their species.
  • Reflex Behaviors: Sudden, involuntary responses to stimuli that do not involve conscious thought. Messages travel only as far as the spinal cord, allowing for rapid reactions.
    • Example 1: When you accidentally touch a hot stove, your hand pulls away instantly, even before you consciously register the pain.
    • Example 2: Blinking your eyes when dust or a sudden bright light appears is an automatic protective reflex.
  • Orientation Behaviors: Movements or postures in response to environmental stimuli.
    • Taxis: A directed movement towards (positive taxis) or away from (negative taxis) a specific stimulus.
      • Example 1 (Negative Phototaxis): Cockroaches scurry away from light when a room is illuminated, demonstrating a negative response to light.
      • Example 2 (Positive Chemotaxis): A male moth flies towards a female moth, guided by the scent of pheromones she releases.
    • Kinesis: Undirected, random movement in response to the intensity of a stimulus, not its direction.
      • Example 1: Woodlice move more rapidly in dry, unfavorable environments until they randomly stumble into a damp, favorable area where they slow down.
      • Example 2: Some invertebrates increase their random movement when exposed to an irritant, dispersing until they find a more comfortable spot.

b. Learned or Acquired Behavior

Learned behavior is not inherited; it is acquired during an individual's lifetime through experience and allows an organism to adapt to changes in its environment.

Types of Learned Behavior:

  • Habituation: A simple form of learning where an animal stops responding to a stimulus after repeated, non-threatening exposure.
    • Example 1: A new puppy might initially bark at every car passing by, but after a few weeks, it learns to ignore the constant traffic noise.
    • Example 2: City birds ignore the constant sound of sirens and human chatter, unlike their rural counterparts who might flee at such noises.
  • Classical Conditioning: A type of associative learning where an animal learns to associate a neutral stimulus with a significant stimulus, leading to a new conditioned response. Discovered by Ivan Pavlov.
    • Example 1 (Pavlov's Dogs): Dogs naturally salivate at the sight or smell of food (unconditioned stimulus). If a bell (neutral stimulus) is consistently rung just before food is presented, the dogs eventually learn to salivate at the sound of the bell alone (conditioned response).
    • Example 2: If a cat hears the distinctive sound of an electric can opener every time it's fed wet food, it may start to run to its bowl and purr when it hears the sound, even if no food is present.
  • Operant Conditioning: A type of associative learning where an animal learns to associate its own voluntary behavior with a consequence (reward or punishment), leading to an increase or decrease in that behavior. Discovered by B.F. Skinner.
    • Example 1 (Skinner Box): A rat in a Skinner box learns to press a lever (behavior) to receive a food pellet (reward), thus increasing the likelihood of pressing the lever again.
    • Example 2: When training a dog, giving it a treat (positive reinforcement) immediately after it sits on command makes the dog more likely to sit when commanded in the future.
  • Insight Learning: A complex form of learning based on past experience and reasoning, allowing an animal to solve problems without trial and error. This is often seen in primates and humans.
    • Example 1: A chimpanzee faced with a banana hanging too high may stack boxes to reach it, demonstrating an understanding of the relationship between the boxes and the banana's height without prior direct instruction.
    • Example 2: When a raven is presented with a piece of food tied to a string, it may quickly pull the string up in segments and hold it with its foot to retrieve the food, rather than randomly pecking at it.
  • Sensitization: A non-associative learning process where repeated exposure to a stimulus results in a progressively amplified response.
    • Example 1: After repeated exposure to a mild electric shock, a sea slug may show an exaggerated withdrawal reflex to even a gentle touch.
    • Example 2: A person living near an airport might initially be startled by airplane noise, but over time, they may become even more sensitive to it, reacting more strongly to each passing plane.

ii. Patterns of Behavior

Animals exhibit various behavioral patterns, some of which are common across many species despite their diversity.

a. Behavioral Cycles:

These are behavioral patterns in which animals respond to periodic changes in the environment, which can be daily or seasonal.

  • Seasonal Migration: The movement of animals from one habitat to another during different times of the year due to seasonal fluctuations in factors like food availability, sunlight, temperature, and breeding conditions.
    • Example 1: Arctic Terns undertake the longest migration of any bird, flying from the Arctic to the Antarctic and back each year to follow summer and abundant food sources.
    • Example 2: Wildebeest in the Serengeti migrate in massive herds across vast plains in search of fresh grazing lands and water, following the seasonal rains.
  • Circadian Rhythms (Biological Clocks): These are approximate 24-hour cycles that regulate various essential functions and processes, like the sleep-wake cycle.
    • Example 1: Humans naturally feel sleepy at night and awake during the day, even in the absence of external cues like light, due to their internal circadian rhythm.
    • Example 2: Nocturnal animals, like owls, instinctively become active at dusk and sleep during the day, guided by their internal biological clock.

b. Reproductive Behavior:

This involves the coordination of timing and patterning of reproductive activity, which is vital for locating and selecting mates, producing offspring, and rearing them successfully.

  • Example 1: Male peacocks display their elaborate tail feathers and perform a courtship dance to attract females.
  • Example 2: Some frog species engage in loud croaking choruses, where males call to attract females during mating season.

c. Social Behavior:

These are behavioral patterns commonly observed in animals that live in groups, often leading to benefits like division of labor or enhanced protection.

  • Example 1: Ants live in highly organized colonies with different castes (workers, soldiers, queen) performing specialized tasks, demonstrating complex social behavior.
  • Example 2: A pack of wolves hunts cooperatively, using coordinated strategies to take down prey much larger than themselves, an example of effective social foraging.

d. Competition:

A behavioral pattern observed when animals compete for limited resources such as space, territory, water, mates, and food.

  • Intraspecific Competition: Competition between individuals of the same species.
    • Example 1: Two male deer lock antlers during the rutting season, fighting for access to females in the herd.
  • Interspecific Competition: Competition between individuals of different species.
    • Example 2: Lions and hyenas frequently clash over carcasses in the savanna, competing for the same food resource.

e. Territoriality:

A behavioral pattern involving protecting a defined space from other animals, ensuring access to resources needed for survival.

  • Example 1: A male lion patrols and marks its territory with urine and roars to warn other lions and predators to stay away.

f. Communication Behavior:

Behavioral patterns vital for interaction among animals, using sight, sound, touch, and chemical cues (pheromones).

  • Example 1 (Chemical Communication): Ants leave a chemical trail (pheromone) from a food source back to their nest, allowing other ants to follow the trail and find the food.
  • Example 2 (Auditory Communication): Birds sing complex songs not only to attract mates but also to warn rivals to stay out of their territory.

2.2.4 Homeostasis in Animals

Homeostasis is the self-regulatory process by which animals maintain stable internal conditions in their bodies despite changes in the external environment. This stability is crucial for optimal physiological function.

Components of Homeostasis:

  • Stimulus: A change in the internal or external environment that triggers a response (e.g., a drop in body temperature).
  • Receptor (Sensor): Detects the change in the environment or body condition (e.g., thermoreceptors in the skin). It sends signals to the control center.
  • Control Center: Receives messages from receptors and sends commands to the effector to counteract the change (e.g., the hypothalamus in the brain).
  • Effector: Acts on the stimulus based on the control center's command, working to return the internal condition to normal (e.g., muscles that shiver, sweat glands that secrete sweat).

i. Thermoregulation

Thermoregulation is the process of maintaining a constant internal body temperature. Animals employ various behavioral, physiological, and morphological strategies to keep their body temperatures within an optimal range.

a. Poikilothermic (Ectothermic) Animals

These animals, also known as cold-blooded animals, lack internal control over their body temperature. Their body temperature generally matches that of their environment. They rely on external sources to regulate their temperature.

Behavioral and Morphological Adaptations:

  • Burrowing: Seeking cooler areas by burrowing into the ground on hot days.
  • Basking: Resting in sunlight on cold days to absorb heat.
  • Seeking Shade/Water: Moving to cooler areas or swimming in water to cool down.
  • Group Activity: Some insects like bees huddle together in a hive to generate warmth in cold seasons.

Poikilothermic Temperature Regulation

Example 1: Lizard Basking

A lizard emerges from its burrow in the morning and lies on a sun-warmed rock. By exposing its body to direct sunlight, it absorbs heat, raising its internal temperature to an active range.

Example 2: Snake Retreating into a Crevice

On an intensely hot afternoon, a snake seeks refuge in a cool, shaded rock crevice. This behavioral response prevents its body temperature from rising to lethal levels.

b. Homeothermic (Endothermic) Animals

These animals, also known as warm-blooded animals, can generate internal heat to maintain a constant internal body temperature, even when the external environment changes.

Physiological and Morphological Adaptations:

  • Insulation: Fur, fat, and feathers trap a layer of air close to the body, reducing heat loss.
    • Example 1: An arctic fox has thick fur and a bushy tail it curls around itself, providing excellent insulation in freezing temperatures.
    • Example 2: Birds fluff up their feathers in cold weather, trapping more air to create an insulating layer against heat loss.
  • Vasoconstriction: Narrowing of blood vessels to the skin reduces blood flow to the body surface, conserving heat.
    • Example 1: When a human is cold, the blood vessels in their skin constrict, making their skin paler and reducing heat loss.
  • Shivering: Involuntary muscle contractions generate heat.
    • Example 2: If a person gets too cold, their muscles begin to shiver, converting chemical energy into kinetic energy and heat, thereby warming the body.
  • Vasodilation: Widening of blood vessels to the skin increases blood flow to the surface, promoting heat loss through radiation and evaporation.
    • Example 1: When a person exercises vigorously and gets hot, their skin flushes red due to vasodilation, allowing excess heat to radiate away.
  • Sweating: Secretion of sweat onto the skin surface allows for evaporative cooling.
    • Example 2: On a hot day, horses sweat profusely. As the sweat evaporates from their skin, it carries away heat, cooling the animal.

The hypothalamus in the brain acts as the primary control center for thermoregulation, receiving temperature signals and activating appropriate responses.

Heat Exchange Mechanisms:

Animals exchange heat with their environment through four main mechanisms:

  • Radiation: Transfer of heat through electromagnetic waves (e.g., heat from the sun).
  • Evaporation: Loss of heat as water changes from liquid to gas (e.g., sweating, panting).
  • Convection: Transfer of heat by the movement of fluids (air or water) over a surface (e.g., wind chill).
  • Conduction: Transfer of heat through direct physical contact (e.g., lying on a cold rock).

Body structure also influences temperature regulation. For instance, large ears (e.g., in elephants) provide a large surface area for heat loss in hot climates, while small ears and thick fur help conserve heat in cold climates.

The surface area-to-volume ratio significantly affects heat loss. Smaller animals have a higher surface area-to-volume ratio, meaning they lose heat more rapidly, leading to higher metabolic rates to compensate. Larger animals have a lower surface area-to-volume ratio, losing heat more slowly.

Surface Area-to-Volume Ratio

Example 1: Rabbit vs. Elephant

A small rabbit has a high surface area-to-volume ratio, meaning it loses heat quickly. To maintain its body temperature, it needs a high metabolic rate and eats frequently. An elephant, being much larger, has a low surface area-to-volume ratio and loses heat slowly, so its metabolic rate per unit mass is lower.

Example 2: Desert Animals with Large Appendages

Many desert animals, like the Fennec fox, have disproportionately large ears. These large ears provide a vast surface area for blood vessels to dissipate excess body heat to the environment, helping to keep the animal cool.

ii. Osmoregulation

Osmoregulation is the process that regulates the osmotic pressure of fluids and the balance of electrolytes in an organism to maintain homeostasis. Water makes up a significant portion of an animal's body (about 60% in humans), distributed between intracellular (inside cells) and extracellular (between cells, blood plasma) fluids. Maintaining the correct balance is vital to prevent imbalances in water movement and electrolyte concentration.

Key Concepts in Osmoregulation:

  • Osmoreceptors: Specialized receptors (e.g., in the hypothalamus and kidneys) that detect changes in osmotic pressure.
  • Hypothalamus and ADH: The hypothalamus in the brain and the Antidiuretic Hormone (ADH) secreted by the pituitary gland play crucial roles in controlling water reabsorption by the kidneys.

Types of Osmoregulation:

  • Osmoconformers: Organisms that maintain their body's osmolarity (solute concentration) to match their surroundings. They do not actively regulate their internal osmotic pressure.
    • Example 1: Marine invertebrates like starfish, jellyfish, and lobsters have body fluids that are isotonic with the surrounding seawater, so they don't expend much energy on osmoregulation.
  • Osmoregulators: Organisms that actively regulate their osmotic pressure independently of the external environment, maintaining a stable internal solute concentration. Most vertebrates, including humans, are osmoregulators.
    • Example 2: Freshwater fish actively take up salts and excrete large volumes of dilute urine to prevent their cells from bursting due to water influx from their hypotonic environment.

The kidney is the main organ for osmoregulation in humans. When water levels are high, the kidneys release a large amount of hypotonic (dilute) urine. When water levels are low, the kidneys retain water and produce a low amount of hypertonic (concentrated) urine, thus maintaining electrolytic balance.

iii. Blood Sugar Regulation

Blood glucose is the primary source of energy for the body's systems, especially the brain. The body continuously regulates glucose concentration to ensure a constant energy supply.

Hormones Involved:

  • Insulin: Produced by the pancreas, insulin is released when blood glucose levels are high. It promotes the conversion of glucose into glycogen (a stored form of glucose) in the liver and muscle cells, thereby lowering blood glucose. It also stimulates glucose uptake by tissue cells.
  • Glucagon: Also produced by the pancreas, glucagon is released when blood glucose levels are low. It promotes the conversion of glycogen back into glucose in the liver, releasing glucose into the blood and raising blood sugar levels.

Blood Glucose Regulation in Action

Example 1: After a Meal

After eating a carbohydrate-rich meal, your blood sugar levels rise. Your pancreas detects this and releases insulin. Insulin signals liver and muscle cells to absorb glucose from the blood and convert it into glycogen for storage, bringing blood sugar back to normal.

Example 2: During Fasting

If you haven't eaten for several hours, your blood sugar levels start to drop. Your pancreas then releases glucagon. Glucagon signals the liver to break down stored glycogen back into glucose, releasing it into the bloodstream to raise blood sugar levels and provide energy.

iv. Control of Homeostasis: Negative and Positive Feedback Mechanisms

Homeostasis is primarily maintained through feedback mechanisms, which are physiological regulation systems that return the body to its normal internal state.

a. Negative Feedback Mechanism

This is the most common type of feedback mechanism in homeostasis. It reverses the direction of a stimulus or any deviation from the normal set point. If a physiological value is too high, negative feedback brings it down; if it's too low, it brings it up.

The negative feedback system involves three basic components: a sensor (receptor) that monitors the physiological value, a control center that compares the value to the normal set point and activates effectors, and an effector that causes a change to reverse the deviation.

  • Example 1 (Temperature Regulation): If your body temperature rises (stimulus), thermoreceptors (sensors) send signals to the hypothalamus (control center). The hypothalamus then signals sweat glands (effectors) to produce sweat, and blood vessels to dilate (vasodilation) to increase heat loss through evaporation and radiation. This cools the body, reversing the initial rise in temperature.
  • Example 2 (Blood Glucose Regulation): When blood glucose levels increase (stimulus), the pancreas (sensor and control center) releases insulin (effector). Insulin causes cells to take up glucose and the liver to convert glucose to glycogen, lowering blood glucose back to normal.

b. Positive Feedback Mechanism

Unlike negative feedback, positive feedback mechanisms accelerate a change in the body's physiological condition rather than reversing it. It pushes the body further away from the normal set point. These are less common for maintaining routine homeostasis but are crucial for processes that need to be rapidly completed.

  • Example 1 (Childbirth): During childbirth, uterine contractions (stimulus) cause the release of oxytocin (effector hormone). Oxytocin intensifies contractions, which in turn causes more oxytocin to be released, creating a positive feedback loop that continues until the baby is delivered.
  • Example 2 (Blood Clotting): When a blood vessel is injured, platelets begin to stick to the injury site (stimulus). These platelets release chemicals that attract more platelets (effector), leading to a rapid accumulation of platelets and the formation of a blood clot, which stops the bleeding.

2.2.5 Renowned Taxonomists in Ethiopia

Professor Sebsebe Demissew has been involved in several successful research projects at universities in Europe and Africa. He has published numerous books and articles on the vegetation and plants of Ethiopia and Africa. He is a member of national and international professional organizations and has served as Chair of the Biological Society of Ethiopia, Secretary-General of the Association for the Taxonomic Study of the Flora of Tropical Africa (AETFAT), and a Council member of the International Association for Plant Taxonomy. He has also served as a director of Flora of Ethiopia and Gulele Botanical Garden.

2.2.6 Renowned Zoologists in Ethiopia

Zoology, the study of animals, encompasses their evolution, anatomy, physiology, behavior, habitats, and health. Ethiopian zoologists have made significant contributions to understanding the diverse animal life within the country. Their work is vital not only for advancing zoological science but also for the economic development of the nation.

Key Concepts Summary

  • The characteristics of living things include movement, respiration, sensitivity, growth, reproduction, excretion, and nutrition.
  • Classification is a method of sorting organisms into a meaningful order, traditionally based on morphology and anatomy, but more recently on DNA (molecular) evidence.
  • The science of naming, identifying, and classifying organisms is known as taxonomy.
  • Carolus Linnaeus (1707–1778), known as the father of taxonomy, introduced a taxonomic hierarchy of classification.
  • The taxonomic hierarchies include domains, kingdom, phylum (division), class, order, family, genus, and species.
  • The three domains are Archaea, Bacteria, and Eukarya (which includes protists, fungi, plants, and animals).
  • Classification provides biologists with a framework to study relationships between living and extinct organisms.
  • Taxonomy is a valuable tool for scientists working in agriculture, forestry, and medicine.
  • The binomial system is an internationally agreed-upon system where an organism's scientific name consists of two parts: the genus and the specific epithet.
  • In scientific names, the first letter of the genus is capitalized, and the specific name starts with a small letter (e.g., Homo sapiens). Scientific names are underlined when handwritten or italicized when printed.
  • Ethiopia has rich biological diversity (biodiversity) due to its geographical location, topographical diversity, and varied climatic features. It is a hotspot for wild plant and animal species with a high degree of endemism.
  • Dichotomous keys are used to identify unfamiliar organisms by providing a choice of two possibilities at each stage.
  • Whittaker's five-kingdom scheme comprises Animals, Plants, Fungi, Monera, and Protista.
  • Monera (bacteria and archaea) typically lack a nucleus or endomembrane system and do not reproduce sexually. Members of both groups generally have cell walls.
  • Protists do not have a specific defining trait; they are a collection of many eukaryotic groups. Most protists are single-celled, but multi-celled and colonial species exist. Their life cycles and nutrition methods vary, with some being autotrophs and others heterotrophs.
  • Fungi are heterotrophs that secrete digestive enzymes to break down organic material and then absorb the released nutrients. Some live as single cells; others grow as a multi-celled mycelium. All fungi have cell walls with chitin.
  • Bryophytes are seedless plants without vascular tissues.
  • Gymnosperms are vascular plants with "naked" seeds.
  • Angiosperms are vascular plants whose seeds are formed in a floral ovary that becomes a fruit; this group includes monocots and dicots.
  • Animals are highly diverse and are generally classified into two groups based on the presence or absence of a backbone: invertebrates (animals with no backbone) and vertebrates (animals with a backbone).
  • Invertebrates include sponges, flatworms, roundworms, annelids, molluscs, and arthropods (e.g., insects, arachnids, crustaceans, and myriapods).
  • Vertebrates include fish, amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals.
  • Dr. Mesfin Taddese, Professor Sebsebe Demissew, Professor Ensermu Kelbessa, Professor Abebe Getahun, and Professor Silesh Nemomissa are some of the renowned Ethiopian taxonomists.
  • Animals are multicellular, eukaryotic, and heterotrophic.
  • The animal kingdom is split into Invertebrates (no backbone) and Vertebrates (have a backbone).
  • Asexual reproduction (single parent) and sexual reproduction (two parents) are both found in the animal kingdom.
  • Insects are vital for pollination and industry but can be pests or disease vectors.
  • Behavior can be innate (genetic) or learned (experience-based).
  • Homeostasis keeps internal conditions like temperature, water, and sugar stable.
  • Negative feedback is the primary way the body maintains balance.

Quiz Complete! 🎉
